1:
"And after these things I saw another angel come down from heaven, having great power; and the earth was lightened with his glory." The brief verse notes the glorious angel announcing Babylon's fall. The grammatical voices note the majestic herald. Barnes and Robertson note the appearance: "another angel come down from heaven, having great power; and the earth was lightened with his glory" — a mighty angel descends from heaven with great power, and the earth is illumined by his glory; the splendor of the angel marks the importance of the announcement (Babylon's fall) he brings; his glory lights the whole earth.

On "having great power," the witnesses note his authority. Barnes and Ellicott note that the angel has "great power (authority)" — a high, mighty angel, fitted to proclaim the doom of the great city; the dignity of the messenger befits the greatness of the message.

On "the earth was lightened with his glory," the latin-fathers Bede and the grammatical-historical note the illumination: "the earth was lightened with his glory" — the angel's brightness fills the earth; whether marking the diffusion of the gospel-light at Babylon's fall, or simply the surpassing glory of the herald; the radiance announces the great proclamation. The verse displays the glorious angel announcing Babylon's fall.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the herald — "another angel come down from heaven, having great power; and the earth was lightened with his glory," the mighty, glorious angel announcing Babylon's fall (Barnes, Robertson); "having great power" the high authority befitting the message (Barnes, Ellicott); "the earth was lightened with his glory," the radiance filling the earth, the gospel-light to some (Bede, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the glorious angel. The verse marks the angel: from heaven. The verse displays the power: great. The verse displays the glory: lighting the earth. The verse displays the message: Babylon's fall. The herald displays the mighty angel proclaiming the great doom. The verse displays the angel: descending. The verse displays the power: mighty. The verse displays the glory: radiant. The glorious angel announcing Babylon's fall displays the opening of the chapter: another angel comes down from heaven with great power, and the earth is lightened with his glory — the majestic herald of the great city's doom. The verse displays the glorious angel, crying Babylon's fall (v.2).
2:
"And he cried mightily with a strong voice, saying, Babylon the great is fallen, is fallen, and is become the habitation of devils, and the hold of every foul spirit, and a cage of every unclean and hateful bird." The witnesses note the proclamation of Babylon's fall and desolation. On "he cried mightily with a strong voice, saying, Babylon the great is fallen, is fallen," the grammatical voices note the announcement. Barnes and Robertson note the cry: "Babylon the great is fallen, is fallen" — the angel proclaims, with a mighty voice, the fall of Babylon; the repetition ("is fallen, is fallen," echoing 14:8; Isaiah 21:9) marks the certainty and completeness of her ruin; the great city is fallen.

On "is become the habitation of devils, and the hold of every foul spirit," the witnesses note her desolation. Barnes and Ellicott note that fallen Babylon "is become the habitation of devils (demons), and the hold (haunt) of every foul spirit" — her ruins are the dwelling of demons and unclean spirits; the figure of utter desolation and abandonment to evil (drawn from Isaiah 13:21-22; 34:11-15, the desolation of old Babylon and Edom); the once-great city becomes a haunt of demons.

On "a cage of every unclean and hateful bird," the grammatical-historical note the ruin: she becomes "a cage (haunt) of every unclean and hateful bird" — the desolate ruins, inhabited only by unclean birds (owls, vultures); the picture of total, loathsome desolation; the proud city reduced to a wilderness of unclean creatures. The verse displays the proclamation of Babylon's fall and desolation.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the cry — "Babylon the great is fallen, is fallen," the certain, complete fall proclaimed (14:8; Isaiah 21:9, Barnes, Robertson); "the habitation of devils... the hold of every foul spirit," her desolation, a haunt of demons (Isaiah 13:21, Barnes, Ellicott); "a cage of every unclean and hateful bird," the loathsome desolation of the ruined city (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the fallen Babylon. The verse marks the cry: she is fallen. The verse displays the desolation: a haunt of demons. The verse displays the spirits: foul. The verse displays the birds: unclean. The proclamation displays the great city ruined and abandoned to evil. The verse displays the Babylon: fallen. The verse displays the devils: dwelling. The verse displays the desolation: loathsome. The proclamation of Babylon's fall and desolation displays the angel's mighty cry: Babylon the great is fallen, fallen, become the dwelling of demons, the haunt of every foul spirit, and the cage of every unclean and hateful bird — the great city reduced to loathsome desolation. The verse displays the fallen city, the cause of her fall declared (v.3).
3:
"For all nations have drunk of the wine of the wrath of her fornication, and the kings of the earth have committed fornication with her, and the merchants of the earth are waxed rich through the abundance of her delicacies." The witnesses note the threefold cause of Babylon's fall. On "all nations have drunk of the wine of the wrath of her fornication," the grammatical voices note her seduction of the nations. Barnes and Robertson note the first cause: "all nations have drunk of the wine of the wrath of her fornication" — the nations have drunk the intoxicating wine of her fornication (14:8; 17:2); she seduced and corrupted all peoples with her idolatry and false worship; her universal corruption is a ground of her judgment.

On "the kings of the earth have committed fornication with her," the witnesses note the rulers' corruption. Barnes and Ellicott note that "the kings of the earth have committed fornication with her" — the rulers entered into corrupt alliance with her (17:2); the kings shared her idolatry and worldly corruption; their fornication with her is a further ground of judgment.

On "the merchants of the earth are waxed rich through the abundance of her delicacies," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note her luxury: "the merchants of the earth are waxed rich through the abundance of her delicacies (luxury, wantonness)" — the merchants grew rich by her excessive luxury and self-indulgence; her wealth and sensuality enriched the traders; the third ground — her proud, luxurious wantonness; the merchants' lament (vv.11-19) follows from their dependence on her trade. The verse displays the threefold cause of Babylon's fall.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the threefold cause — "all nations have drunk of the wine... her fornication... the kings... have committed fornication with her... the merchants... waxed rich through the abundance of her delicacies," the nations seduced, the kings corrupted, the merchants enriched by her luxury (14:8; 17:2, Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, Cyril). The verse displays the grounds of judgment. The verse marks the nations: seduced. The verse displays the kings: corrupted. The verse displays the merchants: enriched. The verse displays the luxury: her delicacies. The causes display Babylon judged for her corruption and luxury. The verse displays the nations: drunk. The verse displays the kings: fornicating. The verse displays the merchants: rich. The threefold cause of Babylon's fall displays the grounds of her judgment: all nations drank the wine of her fornication, the kings committed fornication with her, and the merchants grew rich by her luxury — her corruption of the world and her proud sensuality bring her doom. The verse displays the cause of her fall, with the call to come out of her (v.4).
4:
"And I heard another voice from heaven, saying, Come out of her, my people, that ye be not partakers of her sins, and that ye receive not of her plagues." The witnesses note the call to come out of Babylon. On "another voice from heaven, saying, Come out of her, my people," the grammatical voices note the summons to separation. Barnes and Robertson note the call: "Come out of her, my people, that ye be not partakers of her sins" — a voice from heaven calls God's people to come out of Babylon (echoing Jeremiah 51:6, 45; Isaiah 52:11); the summons to separate from the doomed, corrupt city, lest they share her sins and her judgment.

On "that ye be not partakers of her sins," the witnesses note the danger of complicity. Barnes and Ellicott note the reason: "that ye be not partakers of her sins" — by remaining in Babylon, God's people would lend their sanction to her sins and be implicated in them; the call to separate is a call to purity, to come out from her corruption (cf. 2 Corinthians 6:17); in the historicist reading, the duty to separate wholly from corrupt (papal) Rome.

On "that ye receive not of her plagues," the grammatical-historical note the danger of judgment: "that ye receive not of her plagues" — those who remain in Babylon would share her plagues (vv.8); the call is also for safety — to escape her doom; God calls his own out before the judgment falls, that they perish not with her. The verse displays the call to come out of Babylon.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the summons — "Come out of her, my people, that ye be not partakers of her sins, and that ye receive not of her plagues," the call to separate from doomed Babylon (Jeremiah 51:6; Isaiah 52:11, Barnes, Robertson); "that ye be not partakers of her sins," the call to purity, separation from corrupt Rome to historicists (2 Corinthians 6:17, Barnes, Ellicott); "that ye receive not of her plagues," the call to escape her judgment (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the call to separate. The verse marks the call: come out. The verse displays the people: God's own. The verse displays the danger: her sins. The verse displays the safety: from her plagues. The summons displays God calling his people out of the doomed city. The verse displays the call: come out. The verse displays the sins: shunned. The verse displays the plagues: escaped. The call to come out of Babylon displays God's summons to his people: come out of her, my people, lest you share her sins and receive her plagues — the call to separate from the doomed, corrupt city for purity and for safety. The verse displays the call to separate, her sins reaching to heaven (v.5).
5:
"For her sins have reached unto heaven, and God hath remembered her iniquities." The very brief verse notes Babylon's sins reaching heaven and remembered by God. The grammatical voices note the accumulated guilt. Barnes and Robertson note the cause of judgment: "her sins have reached (clung together, reached up) unto heaven, and God hath remembered her iniquities" — Babylon's sins have piled up to heaven (like the tower of Babel, Genesis 11; cf. Jeremiah 51:9), and God has remembered her iniquities; the accumulated, heaven-high guilt now comes into remembrance for judgment.

On "her sins have reached unto heaven," the witnesses note the heaped guilt. Barnes and Ellicott note that "her sins have reached unto heaven" — her iniquities are so many and so great that they reach to heaven; the figure of an immense, accumulated mass of guilt, crying out for judgment.

On "God hath remembered her iniquities," the grammatical-historical note the divine remembrance: "God hath remembered her iniquities" — God, who seemed to delay, now remembers her sins (16:19); his remembrance issues in judgment; the time of reckoning for her long-accumulated iniquity has come; God does not forget the sins of the persecutor. The verse displays Babylon's sins reaching heaven and remembered by God.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the guilt — "her sins have reached unto heaven, and God hath remembered her iniquities," the heaven-high accumulated guilt now remembered for judgment (Genesis 11; Jeremiah 51:9, Barnes, Robertson); "her sins have reached unto heaven" the immense mass of guilt (Barnes, Ellicott); "God hath remembered her iniquities," God's remembrance issuing in judgment (16:19, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the remembered guilt. The verse marks the sins: reaching heaven. The verse displays the accumulation: heaped high. The verse displays the remembrance: God's. The verse displays the judgment: at hand. The guilt displays Babylon's heaven-high sins brought to reckoning. The verse displays the sins: heaped. The verse displays the heaven: reached. The verse displays the iniquities: remembered. Babylon's sins reaching heaven and remembered by God display the ground of the call to come out: her sins have reached to heaven, and God has remembered her iniquities — the accumulated, heaven-high guilt now brought to reckoning. The verse displays the remembered guilt, with the call for double retribution (v.6).
6:
"Reward her even as she rewarded you, and double unto her double according to her works: in the cup which she hath filled fill to her double." The brief verse notes the call for double retribution on Babylon. The grammatical voices note the just recompense. Barnes and Robertson note the demand for retribution: "Reward her even as she rewarded you, and double unto her double according to her works: in the cup which she hath filled fill to her double" — Babylon is to be repaid as she dealt with others, and doubly, according to her works; the principle of just (indeed redoubled) retribution; she who filled the cup of corruption is to be made to drink a double measure of judgment.

On "Reward her even as she rewarded you," the witnesses note the lex talionis. Barnes and Ellicott note that "reward her even as she rewarded you" calls for retribution in kind — she is repaid for her treatment of God's people; the persecutor is requited; (the call addressed to the agents of judgment, or the saints' vindication).

On "double unto her double... in the cup which she hath filled fill to her double," the grammatical-historical note the doubled measure: "double unto her double... in the cup which she hath filled fill to her double" — the retribution is doubled (Jeremiah 16:18; 50:29), a full and overflowing measure; the cup of judgment is filled double the cup of corruption she filled; the figure of complete, redoubled recompense for her sins. The verse displays the call for double retribution on Babylon.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the retribution — "Reward her even as she rewarded you, and double unto her double according to her works," the just, redoubled retribution on Babylon (Barnes, Robertson); "reward her even as she rewarded you," repayment in kind for her treatment of God's people (Barnes, Ellicott); "double unto her double... in the cup which she hath filled fill to her double," the doubled measure of judgment (Jeremiah 50:29, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the doubled judgment. The verse marks the reward: as she rewarded. The verse displays the measure: double. The verse displays the cup: filled double. The verse displays the works: requited. The retribution displays Babylon repaid double for her sins. The verse displays the reward: returned. The verse displays the double: measured. The verse displays the cup: filled. The call for double retribution on Babylon displays the demand for her judgment: reward her as she rewarded, double to her double according to her works, fill her cup double — the just, redoubled recompense on the corrupt city. The verse displays the doubled judgment, answering her pride (v.7).
7:
"How much she hath glorified herself, and lived deliciously, so much torment and sorrow give her: for she saith in her heart, I sit a queen, and am no widow, and shall see no sorrow." The witnesses note Babylon's pride answered by torment. On "How much she hath glorified herself, and lived deliciously, so much torment and sorrow give her," the grammatical voices note the measured judgment. Barnes and Robertson note the proportion: "How much she hath glorified herself, and lived deliciously (luxuriously, wantonly), so much torment and sorrow give her" — the measure of her self-glorification and luxury is the measure of her coming torment and sorrow; her pride and self-indulgence are repaid with proportionate suffering; the higher she exalted herself, the deeper her fall.

On "for she saith in her heart, I sit a queen, and am no widow," the witnesses note her proud presumption. Barnes and Ellicott note her boast: "she saith in her heart, I sit a queen, and am no widow, and shall see no sorrow" — Babylon presumes in her heart that she reigns securely as a queen, is no bereaved widow, and will never see sorrow (echoing Isaiah 47:7-8, the boast of old Babylon); her proud, false security invites her sudden ruin.

On "shall see no sorrow," the grammatical-historical note the false security: "shall see no sorrow" — she presumes she is beyond calamity, secure in her power and wealth; her confidence that she will never mourn is the height of her pride; this very presumption is answered by the sudden, total judgment (v.8). The verse displays Babylon's pride answered by torment.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the proportion — "How much she hath glorified herself, and lived deliciously, so much torment and sorrow give her," her self-glory and luxury repaid with proportionate suffering (Barnes, Robertson); "she saith in her heart, I sit a queen, and am no widow," her proud presumption of secure reign (Isaiah 47:7-8, Barnes, Ellicott); "shall see no sorrow," her false security inviting sudden ruin (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the proud city judged. The verse marks the pride: self-glorified. The verse displays the luxury: lived deliciously. The verse displays the torment: proportionate. The verse displays the boast: I sit a queen. The judgment displays Babylon's pride answered with sorrow. The verse displays the glory: repaid. The verse displays the queen: presuming. The verse displays the sorrow: coming. Babylon's pride answered by torment displays the measure of her judgment: as much as she glorified herself and lived in luxury, so much torment and sorrow are given her, for she presumed in her heart, "I sit a queen, am no widow, and shall see no sorrow" — her proud false security repaid with sudden ruin. The verse displays the proud city, judged in one day (v.8).
8:
"Therefore shall her plagues come in one day, death, and mourning, and famine; and she shall be utterly burned with fire: for strong is the Lord God who judgeth her." The witnesses note Babylon's sudden and total destruction. On "her plagues come in one day, death, and mourning, and famine," the grammatical voices note the sudden judgment. Barnes and Robertson note the swiftness: "her plagues come in one day, death, and mourning, and famine" — her plagues fall in a single day (suddenly, all at once); death, mourning, and famine come upon her swiftly; the abruptness of her ruin contrasts her presumption of lasting security (v.7).

On "she shall be utterly burned with fire," the witnesses note the burning. Barnes and Ellicott note that "she shall be utterly burned with fire" — the corrupt city is consumed by fire (17:16; 18:9, 18); the complete, fiery destruction of Babylon; the burning marks her total and irreversible ruin.

On "strong is the Lord God who judgeth her," the grammatical-historical note the certainty: "strong (mighty) is the Lord God who judgeth her" — the reason her ruin is sure and total: the God who judges her is mighty; against his strength she cannot stand; her power is nothing before the Almighty; the certainty of the judgment rests on God's might. The verse displays Babylon's sudden and total destruction.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the swiftness — "her plagues come in one day, death, and mourning, and famine," the sudden, simultaneous plagues, contrasting her presumption (Barnes, Robertson); "she shall be utterly burned with fire," the complete fiery destruction (17:16, Barnes, Ellicott); "strong is the Lord God who judgeth her," the certainty resting on God's might (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the sudden ruin. The verse marks the plagues: in one day. The verse displays the judgments: death, mourning, famine. The verse displays the burning: utter. The verse displays the judge: the mighty God. The destruction displays Babylon suddenly and totally ruined by the Almighty. The verse displays the plagues: sudden. The verse displays the fire: consuming. The verse displays the Lord: strong. Babylon's sudden and total destruction displays the swift execution of judgment: her plagues come in one day — death, mourning, and famine — and she is utterly burned with fire, for mighty is the Lord God who judges her; the sudden, complete ruin of the proud city before the Almighty. The verse displays the burned Babylon, lamented by the kings (v.9).
9:
"And the kings of the earth, who have committed fornication and lived deliciously with her, shall bewail her, and lament for her, when they shall see the smoke of her burning," The witnesses note the kings lamenting Babylon's burning. On "the kings of the earth, who have committed fornication and lived deliciously with her, shall bewail her, and lament for her," the grammatical voices note the kings' grief. Barnes and Robertson note the first lament: "the kings of the earth, who have committed fornication and lived deliciously (luxuriously) with her, shall bewail her, and lament for her" — the kings who shared her fornication and luxury now bewail and lament her fall; the first of three laments (kings, merchants, mariners); the partners in her sin mourn her ruin, not in repentance but in self-interest.

On "when they shall see the smoke of her burning," the witnesses note the spectacle. Barnes and Ellicott note that the kings lament "when they shall see the smoke of her burning" — the rising smoke of the burning city (v.8; cf. Genesis 19:28) is the sight that draws their lament; the visible token of her destruction.

On the lament, the eastern Cyril and puritan Henry note its self-interest: the kings mourn her not for her sin but for the loss of the luxury and gain they shared with her; their grief is for themselves, for the pleasures and profits now gone; the lament of those who loved her corruption. The verse displays the kings lamenting Babylon's burning.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the lament — "the kings of the earth, who have committed fornication and lived deliciously with her, shall bewail her, and lament for her," the partner-kings mourning her fall (Barnes, Robertson); "when they shall see the smoke of her burning," the rising smoke drawing their lament (v.8; Genesis 19:28, Barnes, Ellicott); the lament self-interested, for the lost luxury they shared (Cyril, Henry). The verse displays the kings' lament. The verse marks the kings: fornicators with her. The verse displays the lament: bewailing. The verse displays the sight: her burning smoke. The verse displays the motive: self-interest. The lament displays the partner-kings mourning the burning city. The verse displays the kings: weeping. The verse displays the smoke: rising. The verse displays the lament: selfish. The kings lamenting Babylon's burning display the first of the three laments: the kings who shared her fornication and luxury bewail and lament her when they see the smoke of her burning — the partners in her sin mourning her ruin out of self-interest. The verse displays the lamenting kings, standing afar in fear (v.10).
10:
"Standing afar off for the fear of her torment, saying, Alas, alas, that great city Babylon, that mighty city! for in one hour is thy judgment come." The brief verse notes the kings' cry of woe over the fallen city. The grammatical voices note their distant fear. Barnes and Robertson note their posture: "Standing afar off for the fear of her torment, saying, Alas, alas, that great city Babylon, that mighty city!" — the kings stand at a distance, afraid of sharing her torment, and cry their lament; they dare not come near, but mourn from afar; their fear keeps them back even as they grieve.

On "Alas, alas, that great city Babylon, that mighty city," the witnesses note the dirge. Barnes and Ellicott note the cry "Alas, alas (woe, woe), that great city Babylon, that mighty city" — the doubled "alas" is the dirge over the fallen city; they lament the greatness and might now brought to ruin; the contrast of her former glory and present destruction.

On "in one hour is thy judgment come," the grammatical-historical note the suddenness: "in one hour is thy judgment come" — the kings marvel at the swiftness of her fall; in a single hour her judgment came; the abrupt, sudden ruin of the mighty city (the refrain of suddenness, vv.10, 17, 19) astonishes the onlookers. The verse displays the kings' cry of woe over the fallen city.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the distant lament — "Standing afar off for the fear of her torment, saying, Alas, alas, that great city Babylon," the kings mourning from a distance in fear (Barnes, Robertson); "Alas, alas... that mighty city," the dirge over her ruined greatness (Barnes, Ellicott); "in one hour is thy judgment come," the marvel at the sudden ruin (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the kings' dirge. The verse marks the kings: afar off. The verse displays the fear: of her torment. The verse displays the cry: alas, alas. The verse displays the suddenness: one hour. The lament displays the kings mourning the swift fall of the mighty city. The verse displays the kings: distant. The verse displays the woe: doubled. The verse displays the judgment: sudden. The kings' cry of woe over the fallen city displays their distant lament: standing afar off in fear of her torment, they cry "Alas, alas, that great city Babylon, that mighty city! for in one hour is thy judgment come" — mourning the sudden ruin of the mighty city. The verse displays the lamenting kings, before the merchants' grief (vv.11-17).
11:
"And the merchants of the earth shall weep and mourn over her; for no man buyeth their merchandise any more:" The brief verse notes the merchants mourning the loss of their trade. The grammatical voices note the second lament. Barnes and Robertson note the merchants' grief: "the merchants of the earth shall weep and mourn over her; for no man buyeth their merchandise any more" — the merchants weep and mourn over Babylon's fall; the second of the three laments; their grief is for the loss of their trade — no one buys their goods any more now that the great market-city is destroyed.

On "the merchants of the earth shall weep and mourn over her," the witnesses note their lament. Barnes and Gill note that "the merchants of the earth shall weep and mourn over her" — the traders who grew rich by her (v.3) lament her ruin; like the kings, their grief is self-interested, for their own loss.

On "for no man buyeth their merchandise any more," the grammatical-historical note the lost market: "no man buyeth their merchandise any more" — the cause of their grief is plainly the collapse of their trade; with Babylon (their great customer and market) destroyed, their merchandise is unsold; their mourning is for lost profit, not for her sin; the self-interest of the merchants is laid bare. The verse displays the merchants mourning the loss of their trade.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the second lament — "the merchants of the earth shall weep and mourn over her; for no man buyeth their merchandise any more," the merchants grieving the loss of their trade (Barnes, Robertson); "the merchants... weep and mourn over her," the traders enriched by her lamenting her ruin (v.3, Barnes, Gill); "no man buyeth their merchandise any more," the self-interested grief for lost profit (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the merchants' lament. The verse marks the merchants: weeping. The verse displays the grief: over her. The verse displays the cause: no buyers. The verse displays the motive: lost trade. The lament displays the merchants mourning their vanished market. The verse displays the merchants: mourning. The verse displays the merchandise: unsold. The verse displays the loss: their own. The merchants mourning the loss of their trade display the second lament: the merchants weep and mourn over Babylon, for no one buys their merchandise any more — their grief, like the kings', self-interested, for the collapse of their profitable trade. The verse displays the mourning merchants, whose lost goods are listed (vv.12-13).
12:
"The merchandise of gold, and silver, and precious stones, and of pearls, and fine linen, and purple, and silk, and scarlet, and all thyine wood, and all manner vessels of ivory, and all manner vessels of most precious wood, and of brass, and iron, and marble," The brief verse notes the catalogue of Babylon's luxury merchandise. The grammatical voices note the list of costly goods. Barnes and Robertson note the inventory: "the merchandise of gold, and silver, and precious stones, and of pearls, and fine linen, and purple, and silk, and scarlet..." — a long catalogue of the costly, luxurious wares traded by Babylon begins; gold, silver, jewels, pearls, fine fabrics (linen, purple, silk, scarlet), precious woods, ivory, and costly metals and stone; the wealth and luxury of the great market-city.

On the costly fabrics and materials, the witnesses note the opulence. Barnes and Gill note that the list — precious metals, gems, fine cloth, rare woods, ivory, marble — marks the immense luxury and wealth of Babylon; the merchandise of a city given to extravagance and display; her commerce in all things rare and costly.

On the catalogue, the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note its purpose: the detailed inventory (continued in v.13) displays the vast, varied luxury of Babylon and the extent of the merchants' loss; the listing of her wares emphasizes the worldly opulence now brought to nothing; the abundance of her delicacies (v.3) catalogued. The verse displays the catalogue of Babylon's luxury merchandise.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the inventory — "the merchandise of gold, and silver, and precious stones, and of pearls, and fine linen, and purple, and silk, and scarlet...," the catalogue of Babylon's costly, luxurious wares (Barnes, Robertson); the list marking her immense luxury and wealth (Barnes, Gill); the inventory displaying her vast opulence now lost (Cyril, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the luxury goods. The verse marks the merchandise: gold and silver. The verse displays the gems: stones and pearls. The verse displays the fabrics: linen, purple, silk. The verse displays the materials: ivory, wood, marble. The catalogue displays Babylon's vast luxury merchandise. The verse displays the gold: traded. The verse displays the luxury: opulent. The verse displays the wares: costly. The catalogue of Babylon's luxury merchandise displays the wealth of the great market: gold, silver, jewels, pearls, fine linen, purple, silk, scarlet, precious woods, ivory, and costly metals and marble — the vast, varied opulence of the city now brought to nothing. The verse displays the luxury wares, continued in the list (v.13).
13:
"And cinnamon, and odours, and ointments, and frankincense, and wine, and oil, and fine flour, and wheat, and beasts, and sheep, and horses, and chariots, and slaves, and souls of men." The witnesses note the catalogue continued, ending with slaves and souls of men. On "cinnamon, and odours, and ointments, and frankincense, and wine, and oil, and fine flour, and wheat," the grammatical voices note the continued list. Barnes and Robertson note the further wares: "cinnamon, and odours (spices), and ointments, and frankincense, and wine, and oil, and fine flour, and wheat, and beasts, and sheep, and horses, and chariots" — the catalogue continues with spices, perfumes, incense, wine, oil, food, livestock, and chariots; the wares of luxury, religion, food, and transport; the full range of Babylon's commerce.

On "and beasts, and sheep, and horses, and chariots," the witnesses note the livestock and goods. Barnes and Gill note the inclusion of animals, horses, and chariots — the staples and luxuries of trade; the commerce extends from precious goods to beasts and vehicles.

On "and slaves, and souls of men," the grammatical-historical note the climax of the list: the catalogue ends with "slaves (bodies), and souls of men" — Babylon trafficked even in human beings, in slaves and the souls (lives) of men; the climactic, damning item — the trade in human persons; the inhumanity of her commerce, that bought and sold men's bodies and souls; the gravest mark of her corruption. The verse displays the catalogue continued, ending with slaves and souls of men.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the continued list — "cinnamon, and odours, and ointments, and frankincense, and wine, and oil, and fine flour, and wheat, and beasts, and sheep, and horses, and chariots," the further wares of luxury, food, and transport (Barnes, Robertson); the livestock, horses, and chariots among her commerce (Barnes, Gill); "and slaves, and souls of men," the climactic, damning trade in human beings (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the full catalogue. The verse marks the spices: cinnamon, frankincense. The verse displays the food: wine, oil, wheat. The verse displays the livestock: beasts, horses. The verse displays the climax: slaves and souls of men. The catalogue displays Babylon's commerce, even in men's souls. The verse displays the wares: continued. The verse displays the slaves: traded. The verse displays the souls: of men. The catalogue continued, ending with slaves and souls of men, displays the full extent of Babylon's commerce: spices, perfumes, wine, oil, food, livestock, chariots — and, climactically, slaves and the souls of men; the inhuman traffic that marks the depth of her corruption. The verse displays the full catalogue, with the merchants' dirge (vv.14-17).
14:
"And the fruits that thy soul lusted after are departed from thee, and all things which were dainty and goodly are departed from thee, and thou shalt find them no more at all." The brief verse notes the vanished luxuries of Babylon. The grammatical voices note the loss of her delights. Barnes and Robertson note the lament addressed to her: "the fruits that thy soul lusted after are departed from thee, and all things which were dainty and goodly are departed from thee, and thou shalt find them no more at all" — the ripe fruits (the autumn fruits, the choice luxuries) her soul craved are gone; all her dainty and splendid things have departed and will never be found again; the total, irreversible loss of her luxuries.

On "the fruits that thy soul lusted after are departed from thee," the witnesses note the craved delights. Barnes and Ellicott note that "the fruits that thy soul lusted after" — the luxuries and pleasures she craved and gloried in — are now departed; the objects of her sensual desire are taken away.

On "thou shalt find them no more at all," the grammatical-historical note the finality: "all things which were dainty and goodly are departed... thou shalt find them no more at all" — the loss is total and permanent; her splendid, luxurious things are gone forever; the emphatic finality ("no more at all") marks the irreversible ruin of her luxury. The verse displays the vanished luxuries of Babylon.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the loss — "the fruits that thy soul lusted after are departed from thee, and all things which were dainty and goodly are departed from thee," her craved luxuries and splendid things gone (Barnes, Robertson); "the fruits that thy soul lusted after" the pleasures she gloried in, now taken away (Barnes, Ellicott); "thou shalt find them no more at all," the total, permanent, irreversible loss (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the vanished luxuries. The verse marks the fruits: departed. The verse displays the dainties: gone. The verse displays the loss: total. The verse displays the finality: no more at all. The lament displays Babylon stripped of all her delights. The verse displays the fruits: lost. The verse displays the dainties: vanished. The verse displays the finding: never. The vanished luxuries of Babylon display the address to the fallen city: the fruits her soul craved are departed, and all her dainty and splendid things are gone, never to be found again — the total, irreversible loss of her luxury. The verse displays the stripped city, mourned from afar (vv.15-17).
15:
"The merchants of these things, which were made rich by her, shall stand afar off for the fear of her torment, weeping and wailing," The brief verse notes the merchants standing afar in fear and grief. The grammatical voices note their distant lament. Barnes and Robertson note the merchants' posture: "The merchants of these things, which were made rich by her, shall stand afar off for the fear of her torment, weeping and wailing" — the merchants who grew rich by trading with Babylon stand at a distance, afraid of her torment, weeping and wailing; like the kings (v.10), they keep their distance even as they mourn; their grief is for their lost wealth.

On "which were made rich by her," the witnesses note their self-interest. Barnes and Gill note that the merchants "were made rich by her" — their wealth came from her trade (v.3); their lament is for the loss of the riches she brought them; their mourning is self-interested.

On "stand afar off for the fear of her torment, weeping and wailing," the grammatical-historical note their fear and grief: the merchants "stand afar off for the fear of her torment, weeping and wailing" — afraid to come near lest they share her judgment, they mourn from a distance; their weeping and wailing is for themselves, for the loss of their gain; the self-interested lament of the enriched traders. The verse displays the merchants standing afar in fear and grief.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the lament — "The merchants of these things, which were made rich by her, shall stand afar off for the fear of her torment, weeping and wailing," the enriched merchants mourning from a distance in fear (Barnes, Robertson); "which were made rich by her," their wealth from her trade, their grief self-interested (v.3, Barnes, Gill); "stand afar off... weeping and wailing," their fearful, selfish grief (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the mourning merchants. The verse marks the merchants: enriched by her. The verse displays the posture: afar off. The verse displays the fear: of her torment. The verse displays the grief: weeping and wailing. The lament displays the merchants mourning their lost wealth from afar. The verse displays the merchants: distant. The verse displays the riches: lost. The verse displays the wailing: selfish. The merchants standing afar in fear and grief display the second lament's posture: the merchants made rich by her stand far off, afraid of her torment, weeping and wailing — mourning from a distance for their lost wealth. The verse displays the wailing merchants, crying their dirge (vv.16-17).
16:
"And saying, Alas, alas, that great city, that was clothed in fine linen, and purple, and scarlet, and decked with gold, and precious stones, and pearls!" The very brief verse notes the merchants' dirge over the splendid city. The grammatical voices note the lament. Barnes and Robertson note the merchants' cry: "Alas, alas, that great city, that was clothed in fine linen, and purple, and scarlet, and decked with gold, and precious stones, and pearls!" — the merchants, like the kings (v.10), cry "Alas, alas" over the great city; they lament her former splendor — clothed in fine linen, purple, and scarlet, decked with gold, jewels, and pearls (echoing the harlot's array, 17:4); the dirge over her vanished magnificence.

On "that great city, that was clothed in fine linen, and purple, and scarlet," the witnesses note her former splendor. Barnes and Gill note that the merchants lament the city "clothed in fine linen, and purple, and scarlet" — her former luxurious attire, the very wares they sold her; her splendor (drawn from 17:4) is recalled in her ruin.

On "decked with gold, and precious stones, and pearls," the grammatical-historical note the recalled opulence: "decked with gold, and precious stones, and pearls" — the merchants recall her former opulence (the gold and jewels of their trade) now lost; the contrast of her past magnificence and present ruin sharpens their lament; they mourn the splendor that enriched them. The verse displays the merchants' dirge over the splendid city.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the dirge — "Alas, alas, that great city, that was clothed in fine linen, and purple, and scarlet, and decked with gold, and precious stones, and pearls," the merchants lamenting her vanished splendor (17:4, Barnes, Robertson); "clothed in fine linen, and purple, and scarlet" her former luxurious attire (Barnes, Gill); "decked with gold, and precious stones, and pearls," her recalled opulence now lost (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the merchants' lament. The verse marks the cry: alas, alas. The verse displays the city: splendid. The verse displays the attire: linen, purple, scarlet. The verse displays the adornment: gold, jewels, pearls. The dirge displays the merchants mourning the city's vanished magnificence. The verse displays the woe: doubled. The verse displays the splendor: recalled. The verse displays the ruin: lamented. The merchants' dirge over the splendid city displays their lament: "Alas, alas, that great city, clothed in fine linen, purple, and scarlet, decked with gold, jewels, and pearls" — mourning the vanished magnificence of the city that enriched them. The verse displays the lamented splendor, ruined in one hour (v.17).
17:
"For in one hour so great riches is come to nought. And every shipmaster, and all the company in ships, and sailors, and as many as trade by sea, stood afar off," The witnesses note the sudden loss of riches and the mariners' lament. On "in one hour so great riches is come to nought," the grammatical voices note the suddenness. Barnes and Robertson note the marvel: "in one hour so great riches is come to nought" — the merchants marvel that in a single hour such great wealth has come to nothing; the suddenness of the ruin (the refrain, vv.10, 19) astonishes them; the vast riches of Babylon are destroyed in an hour.

On "every shipmaster, and all the company in ships, and sailors, and as many as trade by sea," the witnesses note the third group of mourners. Barnes and Gill note that "every shipmaster, and all the company in ships, and sailors, and as many as trade by sea" — the mariners and seafarers who carried her commerce now join the lament; the third of the three laments (kings, merchants, mariners); all who profited by her sea-trade mourn her fall.

On "stood afar off," the grammatical-historical note their distant grief: the mariners "stood afar off" — like the kings (v.10) and merchants (v.15), they keep their distance, mourning from afar; the seafarers lament the loss of the trade that enriched them; the third group of self-interested mourners stands at a distance. The verse displays the sudden loss of riches and the mariners' lament.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the suddenness — "in one hour so great riches is come to nought," the vast wealth destroyed in an hour, the marvel of the onlookers (vv.10, 19, Barnes, Robertson); "every shipmaster... and as many as trade by sea," the mariners, the third group of mourners (Barnes, Gill); "stood afar off," the seafarers mourning from a distance (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the mariners' lament. The verse marks the riches: come to nought. The verse displays the suddenness: one hour. The verse displays the mourners: shipmasters and sailors. The verse displays the posture: afar off. The lament displays the seafarers mourning the sudden loss of her trade. The verse displays the riches: vanished. The verse displays the mariners: lamenting. The verse displays the distance: kept. The sudden loss of riches and the mariners' lament display the third group of mourners: in one hour the great riches are come to nought, and every shipmaster, sailor, and sea-trader stands afar off — the seafarers joining the lament over the sudden ruin of the city that enriched them. The verse displays the mourning mariners, crying their dirge (vv.18-19).
18:
"And cried when they saw the smoke of her burning, saying, What city is like unto this great city!" The very brief verse notes the mariners' cry at the smoke of her burning. The grammatical voices note their lament. Barnes and Robertson note the cry: "cried when they saw the smoke of her burning, saying, What city is like unto this great city!" — the mariners, seeing the smoke of her burning (v.9), cry out, "What city is like unto this great city!"; they lament her as unequaled, marveling that so great a city is destroyed; the dirge over her incomparable greatness now in ruins.

On "when they saw the smoke of her burning," the witnesses note the spectacle. Barnes and Gill note that the mariners cry "when they saw the smoke of her burning" — the rising smoke (as v.9) is the sight that draws their lament; the visible token of her destruction prompts their cry.

On "What city is like unto this great city," the grammatical-historical note the dirge of incomparability: "What city is like unto this great city!" — the mariners lament her as without equal; the cry (echoing Ezekiel 27:32, the lament over Tyre) marks her as the unrivaled great city, now fallen; the wonder that so incomparable a city should perish. The verse displays the mariners' cry at the smoke of her burning.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the cry — "cried when they saw the smoke of her burning, saying, What city is like unto this great city!" the mariners lamenting her incomparable greatness now ruined (Barnes, Robertson); "when they saw the smoke of her burning," the rising smoke drawing their lament (v.9, Barnes, Gill); "What city is like unto this great city," the dirge of incomparability (Ezekiel 27:32, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the mariners' lament. The verse marks the cry: at her burning. The verse displays the smoke: seen. The verse displays the dirge: what city is like her. The verse displays the wonder: her incomparable greatness. The lament displays the mariners marveling at the unequaled city's fall. The verse displays the smoke: rising. The verse displays the city: incomparable. The verse displays the cry: lamenting. The mariners' cry at the smoke of her burning displays their dirge: seeing the smoke of her burning, they cry, "What city is like unto this great city!" — lamenting the fall of the city they deemed without equal. The verse displays the mariners' wonder, casting dust on their heads (v.19).
19:
"And they cast dust on their heads, and cried, weeping and wailing, saying, Alas, alas, that great city, wherein were made rich all that had ships in the sea by reason of her costliness! for in one hour is she made desolate." The brief verse notes the mariners' mourning and the desolation of the city. The grammatical voices note the gesture of grief. Barnes and Robertson note the mourning: "they cast dust on their heads, and cried, weeping and wailing, saying, Alas, alas, that great city" — the mariners cast dust on their heads (the gesture of deep grief and mourning, Ezekiel 27:30; Lamentations 2:10) and cry "Alas, alas" over the great city; the third lament reaches its climax of grief.

On "wherein were made rich all that had ships in the sea by reason of her costliness," the witnesses note their self-interest. Barnes and Ellicott note that the city was one "wherein were made rich all that had ships in the sea by reason of her costliness (her wealth)" — the seafarers grew rich by her costly trade; their lament, like the others', is for their own enrichment now ended; the mariners mourn the source of their wealth.

On "in one hour is she made desolate," the grammatical-historical note the suddenness: "in one hour is she made desolate" — the refrain (vv.10, 17) of the sudden, swift desolation; the great, rich city is made desolate in a single hour; the abruptness of her ruin marks the closing of the threefold lament. The verse displays the mariners' mourning and the desolation of the city.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the mourning — "they cast dust on their heads, and cried, weeping and wailing, saying, Alas, alas, that great city," the mariners' deep grief (Ezekiel 27:30, Barnes, Robertson); "wherein were made rich all that had ships in the sea by reason of her costliness," their self-interested grief for lost wealth (Barnes, Ellicott); "in one hour is she made desolate," the sudden desolation (vv.10, 17, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the mariners' dirge. The verse marks the gesture: dust on heads. The verse displays the cry: alas, alas. The verse displays the wealth: from her costliness. The verse displays the desolation: in one hour. The lament displays the seafarers mourning the suddenly desolate city. The verse displays the dust: cast. The verse displays the riches: lost. The verse displays the desolation: sudden. The mariners' mourning and the desolation of the city display the climax of the third lament: the seafarers cast dust on their heads and cry "Alas, alas, that great city" that enriched all who had ships by her costliness — for in one hour she is made desolate. The verse displays the desolate city, over which heaven is called to rejoice (v.20).
20:
"Rejoice over her, thou heaven, and ye holy apostles and prophets; for God hath avenged you on her." The witnesses note the call for heaven and the saints to rejoice over Babylon's fall. On "Rejoice over her, thou heaven, and ye holy apostles and prophets," the grammatical voices note the contrast with the earthly lament. Barnes and Robertson note the heavenly summons: "Rejoice over her, thou heaven, and ye holy apostles and prophets" — in pointed contrast to the lament of the kings, merchants, and mariners, heaven and the holy apostles and prophets are called to rejoice over Babylon's fall; the saints and heaven rejoice where the worldly mourn.

On "thou heaven, and ye holy apostles and prophets," the witnesses note the rejoicers. Barnes and Henry note that "heaven, and ye holy apostles and prophets (and saints)" are called to rejoice — the redeemed and the heavenly host, especially the apostles and prophets whom Babylon persecuted; those who suffered from her now rejoice in her judgment.

On "God hath avenged you on her," the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the vindication: "for God hath avenged you (judged your judgment) on her" — God has executed the saints' cause against her, avenging the blood of the persecuted (cf. 6:10; 18:24); the rejoicing is grounded in God's vindication of his people; her fall is the answer to the martyrs' cry. The verse displays the call for heaven and the saints to rejoice over Babylon's fall.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the summons — "Rejoice over her, thou heaven, and ye holy apostles and prophets," heaven and the saints called to rejoice, in contrast to the worldly lament (Barnes, Robertson); "heaven, and ye holy apostles and prophets," the redeemed and persecuted prophets rejoicing (Barnes, Henry); "God hath avenged you on her," God's vindication of his people, answering the martyrs' cry (6:10; 18:24, Henry). The verse displays the call to rejoice. The verse marks the rejoicers: heaven and the saints. The verse displays the contrast: with the mourners. The verse displays the ground: God avenged. The verse displays the vindication: of the persecuted. The summons displays heaven rejoicing at God's vindication. The verse displays the heaven: rejoicing. The verse displays the saints: vindicated. The verse displays the avenging: God's. The call for heaven and the saints to rejoice over Babylon's fall displays the heavenly counterpart to the earthly lament: rejoice over her, heaven and holy apostles and prophets, for God has avenged you on her — the saints rejoicing in God's vindication where the world mourns. The verse displays the rejoicing saints, as the millstone is cast down (v.21).
21:
"And a mighty angel took up a stone like a great millstone, and cast it into the sea, saying, Thus with violence shall that great city Babylon be thrown down, and shall be found no more at all." The witnesses note the millstone cast into the sea, the sign of Babylon's fall. On "a mighty angel took up a stone like a great millstone, and cast it into the sea," the grammatical voices note the symbolic act. Barnes and Robertson note the sign: "a mighty angel took up a stone like a great millstone, and cast it into the sea" — a mighty angel performs a symbolic act (as Jeremiah 51:63-64), casting a great millstone into the sea; the violent, irreversible sinking of the stone signifies the violent, total overthrow of Babylon.

On "Thus with violence shall that great city Babylon be thrown down," the witnesses note the violent fall. Barnes and Ellicott note the interpretation: "Thus with violence shall that great city Babylon be thrown down" — as the millstone is hurled down and sinks, so Babylon will be violently, suddenly cast down; the figure of her sudden, forceful, irreversible destruction.

On "and shall be found no more at all," the grammatical-historical note the finality: "shall be found no more at all" — like the stone sunk in the sea, Babylon will be gone forever, never to be found again; the emphatic finality marks her total, permanent ruin; she vanishes utterly. The verse displays the millstone cast into the sea, the sign of Babylon's fall.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the sign — "a mighty angel took up a stone like a great millstone, and cast it into the sea," the symbolic act of Babylon's violent overthrow (Jeremiah 51:63, Barnes, Robertson); "Thus with violence shall that great city Babylon be thrown down," her sudden, forceful destruction (Barnes, Ellicott); "shall be found no more at all," her total, permanent ruin, gone forever (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the millstone-sign. The verse marks the stone: a great millstone. The verse displays the act: cast into the sea. The verse displays the meaning: Babylon thrown down. The verse displays the finality: found no more. The sign displays Babylon violently and forever cast down. The verse displays the millstone: sunk. The verse displays the city: overthrown. The verse displays the ruin: permanent. The millstone cast into the sea, the sign of Babylon's fall, displays the symbolic act of her doom: a mighty angel hurls a great millstone into the sea, declaring that thus Babylon will be violently thrown down and found no more at all — her sudden, total, irreversible ruin. The verse displays the sunken millstone, with the silence of the desolate city (vv.22-23).
22:
"And the voice of harpers, and musicians, and of pipers, and trumpeters, shall be heard no more at all in thee; and no craftsman, of whatsoever craft he be, shall be found any more in thee; and the sound of a millstone shall be heard no more at all in thee;" The brief verse notes the silence of music and labor in the desolate city. The grammatical voices note the cessation of life. Barnes and Robertson note the desolation: "the voice of harpers, and musicians, and of pipers, and trumpeters, shall be heard no more at all in thee; and no craftsman... shall be found any more in thee; and the sound of a millstone shall be heard no more at all in thee" — the music (harpers, musicians, pipers, trumpeters), the work of craftsmen, and the sound of the millstone (the everyday labor of grinding) all cease in Babylon; the figure of a city utterly desolate, where all the sounds of festive and productive life are silenced (drawn from Jeremiah 25:10; Ezekiel 26:13).

On "the voice of harpers... shall be heard no more," the witnesses note the silenced joy. Barnes and Gill note that the cessation of music marks the end of all festivity and joy in the city; no more songs or celebrations; the silence of a dead city.

On "no craftsman... and the sound of a millstone shall be heard no more," the grammatical-historical note the silenced labor: the absence of craftsmen and the silence of the millstone mark the end of all industry and daily life; no more work, no more bread ground; the city is wholly desolate, devoid of both joy and labor. The verse displays the silence of music and labor in the desolate city.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the silence — "the voice of harpers... shall be heard no more... no craftsman... shall be found... the sound of a millstone shall be heard no more," the cessation of music, work, and daily life in the desolate city (Jeremiah 25:10; Ezekiel 26:13, Barnes, Robertson); the silenced music ending all festivity and joy (Barnes, Gill); the absent craftsmen and silent millstone ending all industry (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the silenced city. The verse marks the music: heard no more. The verse displays the craftsmen: gone. The verse displays the millstone: silent. The verse displays the desolation: total. The silence displays the city devoid of joy and labor. The verse displays the harpers: silenced. The verse displays the craft: ended. The verse displays the millstone: still. The silence of music and labor in the desolate city displays the completeness of Babylon's ruin: the voice of harpers, musicians, and trumpeters is heard no more, no craftsman is found, and the sound of the millstone is silent — the city wholly desolate, devoid of joy and labor. The verse displays the silenced city, dark and joyless (v.23).
23:
"And the light of a candle shall shine no more at all in thee; and the voice of the bridegroom and of the bride shall be heard no more at all in thee: for thy merchants were the great men of the earth; for by thy sorceries were all nations deceived." The witnesses note the darkness and silence of the city and the cause of her fall. On "the light of a candle shall shine no more at all in thee; and the voice of the bridegroom and of the bride shall be heard no more," the grammatical voices note the extinguished life. Barnes and Robertson note the desolation: "the light of a candle shall shine no more at all in thee; and the voice of the bridegroom and of the bride shall be heard no more at all in thee" — no lamp shall shine, and no voice of bridegroom and bride (the sounds of joy, marriage, and home, Jeremiah 25:10) shall be heard; the city is dark and joyless, all light and gladness extinguished; total desolation.

On "for thy merchants were the great men of the earth," the witnesses note her former greatness. Barnes and Ellicott note that "thy merchants were the great men (magnates, princes) of the earth" — her traders were the world's great men; her commerce made her merchants princely; her former greatness and worldly power are recalled in her ruin.

On "by thy sorceries were all nations deceived," the grammatical-historical note the cause: "by thy sorceries (enchantments, magic arts) were all nations deceived" — Babylon deceived all nations by her sorceries; whether literal magic or the figure of her seductive, corrupting deceptions (her false worship, allurements, and intrigues); the cause of her judgment is her deception and corruption of the nations. The verse displays the darkness and silence of the city and the cause of her fall.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the desolation — "the light of a candle shall shine no more... the voice of the bridegroom and of the bride shall be heard no more," the dark, joyless, silent city (Jeremiah 25:10, Barnes, Robertson); "thy merchants were the great men of the earth," her former worldly greatness (Barnes, Ellicott); "by thy sorceries were all nations deceived," her deception and corruption of the nations the cause of her fall (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the darkened city. The verse marks the light: extinguished. The verse displays the joy: silenced. The verse displays the merchants: great men. The verse displays the cause: her sorceries. The desolation displays the city dark and judged for her deceptions. The verse displays the candle: dark. The verse displays the bride: silent. The verse displays the sorceries: deceiving. The darkness and silence of the city and the cause of her fall display the completeness of her ruin and its ground: no lamp shines, no voice of bridegroom and bride is heard; her merchants were the great men of the earth, but by her sorceries she deceived all nations — the dark, silent city judged for her deceptions. The verse displays the judged city, in whom the blood of the saints was found (v.24).
24:
"And in her was found the blood of prophets, and of saints, and of all that were slain upon the earth." The witnesses note the blood of the saints found in Babylon. On "in her was found the blood of prophets, and of saints," the grammatical voices note her bloodguilt. Barnes and Robertson note the final charge: "in her was found the blood of prophets, and of saints, and of all that were slain upon the earth" — Babylon is found guilty of the blood of the prophets and saints; she is the great persecutor, in whom the blood of God's slain people is found; the chief ground of her judgment is her bloodguilt against the faithful.

On "the blood of prophets, and of saints," the witnesses note her persecution. Barnes and Gill note that "the blood of prophets, and of saints" — the martyred prophets and faithful — is found in her; she killed God's people (17:6; 18:20); her persecution of the saints brings her judgment.

On "and of all that were slain upon the earth," the grammatical-historical note the comprehensive guilt: she is guilty of "all that were slain upon the earth" — the blood of all the martyrs, indeed of all unjustly slain, is charged to her (as Jerusalem in Matthew 23:35); the great persecuting power is held responsible for the blood of God's people through the ages; her comprehensive bloodguilt seals her doom. The verse displays the blood of the saints found in Babylon.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the bloodguilt — "in her was found the blood of prophets, and of saints, and of all that were slain upon the earth," Babylon guilty of the blood of the martyrs, the great persecutor (17:6; 18:20, Barnes, Robertson); "the blood of prophets, and of saints," the martyred faithful slain by her (Barnes, Gill); "all that were slain upon the earth," her comprehensive guilt for the blood of God's people (Matthew 23:35, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the bloodguilty city. The verse marks the blood: of prophets and saints. The verse displays the guilt: found in her. The verse displays the scope: all the slain. The verse displays the ground: her persecution. The charge displays Babylon judged for the martyrs' blood. The verse displays the blood: found. The verse displays the saints: slain. The verse displays the guilt: comprehensive. The blood of the saints found in Babylon displays the final ground of her judgment: in her was found the blood of prophets, saints, and all the slain upon the earth — the great persecutor, guilty of the martyrs' blood, justly judged. The verse displays the bloodguilty Babylon, over whose fall heaven cries Alleluia (ch.19).
